content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions month

## 1. Looking for movie recommendations for my 23F daughter who’s lost her will to live— something visually beautiful, maybe with nature, or small moments that gently remind you why life is worth living.”

**Score:** 601
**Body:** She’s 23 and has been going through a phase where she feels really disconnected from life. It’s not dramatic or loud, more like a quiet loss of excitement, curiosity, and meaning. Things that used to bring her joy don’t really land the same anymore. Nothing seems to exite her anymore.I’ m not looking forything overly heavy or preachy. Movies that make you pause, breathe, and feel something again. I cannot bear to see her like this so any suggestion will be helpful 

Edit: yes she is going to therapy. I don't want to force her into anything. She decided to go and I remain supportive of whatever help she needs. I just want to be there for her through the highs and the lows.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Marcel the Shell With Shoes On.
2. Flow. No talking. Just a cat and other animals caught in a flood and then going down a river as unlikely friends. Just beautiful. 

Just in case you haven’t - be sure she sees a psychiatrist. Sometimes prescription meds can help balance the mind until things get settled. Chemical imbalance is a real thing and it isn’t about just working through it
3. Amelie, About Time. 
  - I’ll second Amelie. One of the most beautiful and feel good movies ever made.
4. I really like The Secret Life of Walter Mitty for this
5. Little Miss Sunshine 
6. The Station Agent.  It's about a man who is almost completely isolated/solitary and likely depressed who gradually develops community.  A slow and sweet film.
7. Perfect Days by Wim Wenders - it's on Hulu!

It's about a man in Tokyo who happily cleans bathrooms for a living (some of the most beautiful and unique bathrooms I've ever seen too). He doesn't have many friends and is just kind of reading and listening to music and being benevolent to strangers he meets. 

It was so soothing watching someone live a very mundane life and feel content, peaceful, and satisfied with it. It's very slow paced and has an awesome soundtrack bc the main character is a musichead lmao. It's what aspire to live like once my ego finally dies and i can just have gratitude for my life all the time 😎
8. Studio ghibli films
9. Hope your daughter feels better soon 🫂

## 2. I just watched Downfall (2004) last night, and it was amazingly effective at showing how many Nazis were true believers to the very end.

**Score:** 375
**Body:** Downfall (2004) is a historical docudrama showing the final 10 days of World War II from the perspective of people in Adolf Hitler’s bunker. It’s very realistic, and it shows what the final days might have looked like when Hitler realized he would lose World War II.

It showed how vindictive and self destructive Hitler was, as well as how much backstabbing took place between his top Nazi generals. Towards the end, Hitler declared that he didn’t care if millions of civilians died, because “there are no non-combatants in total war.” He also blamed the German people for electing him, and he ordered Germany’s civilian infrastructure to be destroyed. He also believed that the non-Nazi survivors should be killed because they are weak compared to his Nazi supporters.

I think it’s an important film to watch because it shows just how demented evil people in power can be and what a cornered dog looks like in the last days of his life. It reminds me of some political leaders in charge today, too.

Highly recommend!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would also recommend Japan's Longest Day which shows how many Japanese were true believers to the very end of WW2.

A little known history - there was actually a failed coup in Japan trying to stop Japan surrender after the atomic bombs.
2. The Zone of Interest is also a good film. It highlights how ordinary people were complicit in what happened during the war
3. That's an amazing movie. If you get the chance watch the extended cut it has 25 more intense minutes.  Bruno Ganz did a great job, the entire cast was excellent 
4. I remember when it was released people were really angry that it humanised Hitler.
  - That was kind of a dumb take on their part because he was, in fact, human. 
5. [removed]
6. Yes, I was struck by how at the end it’s unthinkable to the characters that they might have to face a world without National Socialism.

Also, the fate of the Goebbels kids 🌚
  - Yes- the Goebbels family scene was the most horrifying scene. Frau Goebbels “lovingly” administered neurotoxins to her children, killing them in their sleep. It’s a brutal movie, but I think the brutality is important to witness, because it really actually happened.
7. No respect for human life, no sense of basic survival. Running around demanding suicide, committing suicide, first shooting everyone they know, and killing their own children. Shocking to see that level of total absence of the survival instinct. The film was mostly about those in the bunker w hitler at the end, as i recall, not the general public. I understand the general public was all for surviving,  if they could. 
  - Even as the Russians were approaching, there were people in Berlin shooting civilians for fleeing.

Humans can become really insane.

## 3. 10 Cloverfield Lane, now 10 years old, is one of the best thrillers of the last decade

**Score:** 380
**Body:** If you've never seen it before, I highly recommend it.

About a young woman (Mary Elizabeth Winstead) who survives a car crash, and wakes up to find herself in an underground bunker with two men (John Goodman, and John Gallagher Jr.) who insist that some event has left the surface of Earth uninhabitable.

That's all I'll say. If you can, try to go into the movie cold. 
10 Cloverfield Lane (2016) is an intense gem of 2010's cinema, excellent performances, and an intriguing atmosphere.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Such a good movie.  John Goodman is so good at playing someone who can both be seemingly helpful but slightly off and then switching to 100% intimidating.  
  - Absolutely, i feel another actor that’s great at this is John Carroll Lynch
    - Good one.  Loved him in Zodiac 
2. It's part of a trilogy, three different perspectives of the same event. 

*Cloverfield* (2008) depicts "the event" from the streets of New York City as it unfolds.

*The Cloverfield Paradox* (2018) takes place in a space station before "the event", and shows what caused it. 

*10 Cloverfield Lane* (2016) set in Lake Charles, LA shows how people are coping with "the event". It takes place at a later time than the other 2 movies, which happen at about the same time. 

They're all JJ Abrams/Bad Robot productions and are all good science fiction movies.  
  - I watched Cloverfield Lane completely unaware of any connection, and never seeing the other two. I’m so glad I did. It was such a great mindfuck. 
    - Have always said it should have been standalone. It’s the best of the Cloverfield movies by a MILE
      -  I think it was originally written as a standalone and only much later in the process tied into Cloverfield. 
3. If you liked this, also check out Hidden (2015).
4. Dan Trachtenberg made his directorial debut with this movie
5. Creepy and dark, fantastic film

## 4. What would a 6-year-old girl, 10-year-old boy, and 67-year-old man enjoy watching together?

**Score:** 345
**Body:** The young ones were interested in Zootopia and K-pop Demon Hunters, but grandpa didn't care for them.  Grandpa proposed Babes in Toyland (1961), but the children had watched it within the past couple of years, and did not want to watch it again.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Up
  - Came to say this one!

Also: 

The Road to El Dorado

How To Train Your Dragon 

The Emperor’s New Groove

Kung Fu Panda 

Hook 
    - I don't care what age you are, The Emperor's New Groove is perfect! It always puts me in a good mood.
2. Secondhand Lions
3. The Princess Bride.   - my grandson didn't want to watch it because of the word Princess, but he loved it.
Journey to the Center of Earth 
4. The Wild Robot!! Sooo good
5. The Iron Giant?
  - As a 67-year-old, can confirm Grandpa should enjoy it. The setting is actually just before when I was a kid -- '50s, not '60s -- but the vibe is like some of the shows I grew up watching.
6. Toy Story

Toy Story 2

Monsters Inc

The Princess Bride
7. The Sandlot

## 5. I’m in the mood for a life changing movie: what should I watch?

**Score:** 327
**Body:** I’ll set the scene. I just got off from a grueling shift at work. I was freezing the whole shift. I’m now cozy in my bed and I want to watch a movie that will make me forget im alive and let me zero-in on the screen. Sort of like an Interstellar type of thing. Please help. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. City of God
2. The fall.  
  - I can’t believe this. I watched two movies today staying home sick and those were the first two mentioned here (The Game was the other). Sicario is next.
    - Sicario is great
3. The Game
4. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty 
5. Dances With Wolves 
6. Terminator 1+2
  - You might’ve hit the mark with this one. Terminator is sounding reeeeal good right now.
7. The Life of Chuck

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic month

## 1. Denis Villeneuve casually dropped a whole trilogy between 5 years.

**Score:** 17356

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Time for a remake. First one is over half a decade old. 
  - Dune the TV series brought to you by Amazon starring Shaquille O'Neal as Paul Atreides. 
    - Zendaya plays the same part 😂
      - I would love to see Terry Crews taking up the role instead, he would make a banger Zendaya
2. Kinda funny when TV shows wait a good 4 years in between seasons these days….
  - And it's just 8 episode seasons as opposed to 20+ that used to be the norm.
    - I read somewhere that the low episode count these days is a way to dodge having to use a writer's union. 
3. This seems fairly normal for trilogies. Often you’d see two year gaps between each, so if anything the 3 year gap from ‘21 to ‘24 makes this a little longer than a normal quick turnaround trilogy.

But the quality and confidence of vision demonstrated by these films is praiseworthy regardless of the release timeline. 

  - How many planned trilogies are released over more than a decade? You film them while you still have the cast under contract and not significantly aged between releases. 
4. Is that an achievement? Lotr trilogy was dropped in 3years

## 2. Ralph Fiennes doesn’t just act. He disappears into every role.What is your favorite?

**Score:** 16100

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He was just too delightful in The Grand Budapest Hotel as Gustave.
  - “too delightful” is definitely a phrase M Gustave would use to describe someone. He was so perfect. 
    - https://i.redd.it/ntfdg4hmi6tg1.gif
      - GET YOUR HANDS OFF MY LOBBY BOY! 
  - "You're a real straight guy"  
"Well I've never been accused of that before"  
So funny
2. He scared the shit out of Spielberg when he auditioned for Schindler's List...
3. He's amazing in bone temple
4. Chef Slowik in The Menu
5. I'm Bruges 
6. Maid In Manhattan. One of the great miscasts in cinema. Everyone else plays it like the romantic comedy it’s meant to be but Fiennes is channeling Patrick Bateman with an English accent despite being the romantic lead. 

He’s still Ralph Fiennes though so his terrible casting and inappropriate performance still somehow elevates the film. Glorious.

## 3. What is the scariest, most unsettling shot you’ve ever seen?

**Score:** 10908
**Body:** What is that one movie shot that’s so unsettling and chilling, it’s the one shot you never forget?

For me, it’s the Giant Man scene from It Follows. Man this shot is so nerve wracking!

The aesthetic and dark hollow eyes are something I will never forget.

What’s that one shot for you? Horror movie or otherwise.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The girl under the sheets in the grudge. As if she isn’t scary enough, she gets in bed with you too. Like what the fuck. Kid rules, clearly state. We are supposed to be safe when we pull the sheet on over our faces.
2. The scene in Parasite where the squatter slowly pops his head up in the stairs.
3. https://preview.redd.it/cj0m1pld0uvg1.png?width=1200&format=png&auto=webp&s=cd48b4c21267f5c231378826be49e210bb4fb0f9

No image has ever haunted me the way this one did. I saw Blair Witch in theaters as a kid, used to go to the woods near Burkittsville for camping.

Even just googling for it gives me the willies.
  - This isn't for me the scariest shot personally, but its my favorite in all of horror. So unsettling, such a great "oh fuck" moment as you recall the one single interview at the beginning of the movie that sets it up, ths unnaturalness of it.    
The whole Parr house sequence is imo the best sequences in all of horror and this ending is monumentally impactful. 

4. the first time you see the monsters in The Descent  

https://preview.redd.it/67l8qsg7ztvg1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=0c6c0e6059ef904fa159bb6ed88accc1f3866b62


5. https://i.redd.it/vbp730w8ttvg1.gif

This shot from The Babadook got me pretty good
6. Silence of the lambs when Clarice was walking around and wild Bill‘s house and couldn’t see and he had on the night vision goggles has always haunted me! 
7. ![gif](giphy|SMEDDr3CIB7s4)

Obviously not the scariest, but that shit scared the crap outta me and all my friends when it happened.
8. The girl in the closet in the Ring for just a second. Terrifying.
9. ![gif](giphy|hfKxK1wWDxdO8)

This, when I was a kid.

## 4. Hugo Weaving in V for Vendetta is one of the best performances where an actor doesn't show his face and still crushed it.

**Score:** 9341
**Body:** Hugo is a fantastic actor but what he does in V for Vendetta is on a whole other level.

And the fact that he replaced James Purefoy six weeks into production is insane!

His line delivery and voice is so perfect, you feel every emotion just from him speaking and the mask never hinders his performance.

One of the best film performances where the actor never shows their face and still knocks it out of the park.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The voice inflection and head and body movements made up for lack of expression.  He was amazing in V for Vendetta.  One of my favorite movies.  
  - You hit the nail on the head. Every gesture and movement felt so in character.

We didn’t even need to see his face. The voice alone carried his performance. 
2. ![gif](giphy|PwhzKJ3N6cLFS)
  - Jackie Earle Haley is an absolute scene stealer here. One of my favorite performances of the 2000’s. 
3. Absolutely agree.

Edward Norton is another contender for great masked performance.

![gif](giphy|3DfDVbmubDcjsUkOoI)
  - 
“When I was sixteen, I won a great victory. I felt in that moment I would live to be a hundred. Now I know I shall not see thirty. None of us know our end, really, or what hand will guide us there. A king may move a man, a father may claim a son, but that man can also move himself, and only then does that man truly begin his own game. Remember that howsoever you are played or by whom, your soul is in your keeping alone, even though those who presume to play you be kings or men of power. When you stand before God, you cannot say, "But I was told by others to do thus," or that virtue was not convenient at the time. This will not suffice. Remember that.”


- King Baldwin IV
4. ![gif](giphy|l3diLV2QHVzh3koJa)
5. ![gif](giphy|CbYJLnm37JMre)
  - https://i.redd.it/x0olgkhodgtg1.gif

Karl Urban is incredible
6. ![gif](giphy|Gj4LjEGMVqCotiKZc8)

## 5. Why wasn’t Noomi Rapace a bigger actress?

**Score:** 8780
**Body:** She started out starring in the Swedish The Girl with Dragon Tattoo series then had some huge mainstream roles in Prometheus and Sherlock Holmes then did a bunch of lousy streaming/ straight to DVD flicks. Just re-watched Prometheus and was thinking why she didn’t become a more prominent actress. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. She’s pretty big in Sweden
  - She was great in Svart Krabba. The plot sounds silly, but it’s a fun action movie. 

“A former speed skater turned soldier, who is recruited for a covert mission known as "Svart krabba." Alongside five other soldiers, she must skate across a frozen archipelago during an endless winter to deliver a mysterious package that could end the war.”
  - She was absolutely fantastic in Girl with a dragon tattoo 
  - She was great in Lamb

https://preview.redd.it/c9usf5qrs6rg1.jpeg?width=257&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=1bb05f5f19acf8aae080cee581ad57cec222f132
2. It's fairly common for actors to become massive for a few years and a few roles, then vanish to obscurity.
  - Exactly. It’s an incredibly competitive business to be a lead in major movies. She’s accomplished more than 99% of actors out there
3. Loved her in What Happened to Monday
4. she stars in English and Swedish speaking movies The trip was decent
  - Yeah this is it specifically. She splits her time between two different countries major film roles and every actor who does that doesn’t dominate American movies. She is very famous she just isn’t a Hollywood megastar because she does 60% Swedish films 
5. She’s big in Sweden where she’s from, maybe her goal wasn’t to conquer the American market? 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm month

## 1. Sinners and it’s incredibly clumsy, half-baked second act

**Score:** 1177
**Body:** Whenever people criticise Sinners, a lot of the critiques seem to suggest that the movie falls apart in it’s third act, in the battle between the humans and the vampires. And while I have my own gripes with the third act, to me, the most problematic part of the movie is the second act: after the Vampires are revealed. 

Even by the standards of any horror film, the second-act has always stood out to me as being clumsy and half-baked. The entire second act feels incredibly low in tension, and Coogler seems to have very little ideas about how to fill up the runtime than to repeat a bunch of scenes of people asking to be let inside the juke joint. 

When I first watched the movie in cinemas, I got whiplash when the climax kicked in, because I simply couldn’t believe that the entire second act was wasted on alternating between scenes of Vampires asking to be let inside, and some menial conversations between the survivors, most of which feel like afterthoughts. Sammy, who is the heart of the first half, might as well be relegated into a supporting character in the second act. For a movie that has such a throbbing heart in the first and third act, the second act has always stood out to me as being lethargic and low on any movement or kinetic energy.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I mostly really liked the movie, but I was annoyed at how dumb it seemed to think I was. It opens with narration about how some people can play music that bridges that transcends time, then repeats that narration during the "I Lied to You" scene in case we forgot. When Annie's dying and says "I've got someone waiting for you" it flashes back to their child's grave. Like dude, I'm following along just fine thanks.
  - They also did the flashbacks to the brothers buying the property when they reveal they were set up by the klan.

I also thought the epilogue was a really clumsy way to spell out the themes when the movie did a great job showing them already
    - A bunch of scenes for stupid people, essentially. It's embarrassing how much this happens in modern films, I'm tired of them clearly being made in a boardroom and some suit making sure idiots and people on their phones can follow along.
  - YES. I felt exactly this way too. I like Coogler but he treats his audience like we’re idiots. 
    - It’s the always on their phone people.  The movies have to play well in the theater.  And then on streaming, people with the attention span of squirrels need the constant reminder of plot points.
      - It’s creeping in everywhere - there’s a new Count of Monte Cristo on masterpiece theater it had not one but two flashbacks in the SAME EPISODE of stuff that happened IN THAT EPISODE. 
    - Not written like that in the script. It’s likely product of testing the film and dumb audiences not understanding clearly. 
    - I feel like these things are habits from making MCU movies 
2. I really was hoping for more as well. Especially when it came to the connection between the church, sin, music, and demons. I was hoping for more of Sammy’s connection with religion and his pastor father. And it was framed like that in the beginning too when Sammy is rushing into his father’s congregation, implying this would be an important theme by the end. 

The KKK plot came out of almost nowhere and left me cold. I think they couldn’t figure out how to connect the themes at the end. 
  - Yeah the movie sets up in its prologue AND in its standout sequence that music can open a doorway between the veil and connect the last, present and future. And spends so much time setting up Sammie as having a gift that seems almost God-given, but instead of using it in his father’s church, he’s using it for a very different kind of spiritual experience.

So clearly the film is setting up a battle that uses the supernatural quality of his music for the end climax right? Especially after showing the vampires also having a spiritual connection to music and ability to share each other’s memories and culture, right?

Oh no, the end is just a sloppy fight scene with questionable character choices, poorly choreographed action and a needlessly bloody massacre of the KKK tacked on for some reason. What a letdown.

The best spectacle films use things like musical/action sequences to both further the plot and tell you about our characters. This film does neither. And it jams so many ideas in there that it doesn’t know what to do with them, so just abandons them in favour of a very unsatisfying genre finale. Which is especially egregious, because there is a rich history of horror films that use the “overnight siege by supernatural forces” trope far better and more compellingly tense.

Instead, this film goes out of its way to undermine its own stakes. Passing-around-the-garlic sequence to find out who is the secret vampire, similar to the infamous sequence in The Thing must be similarly tense right? Oh wait, no one’s a vampire, so no.

The twins having double-crossed both sides in Chicago is surely setting up a reckoning where angry gangsters come after them, right? Nope, never becomes an issue.

Even relegating the KKK attack to some weird addendum that you could cut out of the film to literally no sequence sucks the dramatic possibilities out of the story. Why not have the KKK come after them during the middle of the night, to burn down the barn and cause maximum casualties? Then the vampires could attack them, and you have supernaturally powered KKK dudes. Or they could have a three-way battle? Pretty much any other option would be far more intense and dramatic than anything the film attempts.

## 2. Am I wrong if I feel like the second half of Sinners lessens the movie for me?

**Score:** 950
**Body:** the first half was tremendous, a complete mastercraft in character, atmosphere etc. but once it gets to the vampires I completely shut off my brain. I’m not one of those people that think the movie should have done without the vampire, I just don’t think Coogler made them that compelling. At no point did they ever feel like a threat or unique in any way, and the final battle was just clumsy. I can’t just avoid this because it’s half the film, it does lessen it for me in the long run. anyone feel the same. I want to love this movie, but I end up just liking it

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t mind the vampires, but you’re definitely right about the final battle feeling clumsy. When the store owner woman finally breaks and invites the vampires in, the action looks really rough. All I remember thinking is how bad it looked in that flurry of stuff going on. Still a great movie, but it would be a lot better if they nailed that action to make it more exciting because as is it is a let down. 
  - The random red shirts from inside the club that showed up at the end just to be killed is just lazy film making. Like we had 30 minutes of indoor scenes without them showing up and they are just there at the door to be the first ones killed. Also the continuity of the scene is just a mess, makes no sense how that fight would play out, a lot of suspension of disbelief is needed during this scene. 

I don't know what is up with Coogler and his final big action scenes but this is the second time he fucks it up imo, the one at Black Panther 1 was egregious. Will he have a introspective look into his own work and notice a pattern? Maybe, but he was being rewarded left and right for both of those movies so...
  - Yeah, I can’t see how anyone really thinks much of that final battle.  It’s just objectively clumsy and makes no real sense.

I still like the movie and don’t think the final battle takes away much.  But it sure could have been done better
2. agree, the film opening with the guy entering the church all bloody, the scene with the indigenous hunters chasing the vampire, introduction of all the side characters like the store owners and their dynamic with smoke and stack, such great atmosphere and world building only to devolve into a campy vampire horror flick. if coogler maintained the more serious tone like the opening scene, the film would have felt more cohesive and not so disjointed like two different movies in one. 
  - Totally agree. The action was so corny, like when they finally "invite" the vampires in, and then they all have to grab their own unique makeshift weapon and pose for the group shot, ready to fight....like it's a scene from the Avengers or something
    - And then extras just spawn randomly so they can get killed and somehow 30+ vampires don’t overwhelm all of them immediately
    - That particular shot sticks in my head as an annoyance. In my memory, it’s an awkward discontinuous cut into it too (though I’ll have to watch again to confirm). Which is not necessarily a bad thing, but it was a bad sort of jarring for me in this case.

Everything leading up to that suggested that the humans would be instantly eviscerated, which completely distracted me from the fight. Meanwhile the fight itself is a little rough in the choreography.

And like OP, I was really impressed by the first half. I’m not even down on it overall. But it’s definitely a bit of a letdown for me. Maybe I’ll feel a little more soft on it during rewatch, with my expectations set correctly.
3. My problem with the final battle was Coogler shot it like a Marvel movie. The camera is zooming around, picking up a character to do a cool kill, then moving off to the next person. The issue is these aren’t superheroes. These are real characters I’m very invested in who I’m pretty sure don’t know how to take a vampire in hand to hand combat. Every time the camera leaves one person to go check in on someone else, I had the thought of “wait, though, is that person ok?” 

The concept of “look at these badasses fighting a whole mob of vampires” just feels out of sync with everything else the movie is trying to say. And the real stark contrast is the final scene with the KKK, because that has absolutely earned the “look at this badass killing KKK members” tone it takes on.
4. you can’t be wrong over personal opinions about art but I definitely agree in this instance. the scene with the garlic test made me feel like I was watching a high school drama interpretation of John Carpenter’s The Thing.
  - I love that scene conceptually, but I just don't understand why Delta Slim suddenly turns into a Loony Tunes character in the second half. The 'I just shit myself' joke, the random throwaway line about the 'vampire' who sucked his dick, and his overblown reaction to the garlic are all so weird.

## 3. Project Hail Mary (2026): I enjoyed this movie! However, I would have enjoyed it more if it wasn't chock full of cutesy cutesy humor [ spoilers]

**Score:** 644
**Body:** **Spoilers for this movie**

I feel like superhero cutesy humor dropped into the middle of very high stakes drama is sort of infecting the whole movie landscape now. Case in point... project Hail Mary. 

This is a perfectly enjoyable flick! I sat through it and thoroughly had a great time. However the constant and never ending cutesy humor really took me out of the moment multiple times it honestly got very annoying. For example when grace was telling rocky about the fact that his girlfriend now lived with somebody else named Dave and rocky responds with “I hate Dave”. And of course it's supposed to be very funny and I'm sure most people did think it's funny, but to me it's just sort of eye rolling. 

For one thing rocky didn't have the personality of an alien. He had the personality of a human being. 100% very human. He looked like an alien but his psychology was totally human. And all of the “fist my bump” stuff too. I guess it's amusing but it just felt like this movie was constantly trying to shoe horn in jokey jokey laugh a minute type situations that were very jarring with its actual subject matter of the literal destruction of the earth itself.

 A little bit of humor is definitely great to crack up a drama. But when it's just never ending constant little quips and jokes to me it gets old, especially when the nature of the jokes are very self aware and and honestly way too cute.

If the credits rolled on this movie and it said “directed by James Gunn” I wouldn't have been surprised. Because that's the type of humor we have here. I just wish it would've been more in the vein of “arrival” and less in the vein of guardians of the Galaxy

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The humor in the movie was actually toned *down* from the novel. Andy Wier writes one character well: middle-aged sarcastic White dude. It's clearly an idealized version of himself. So, any character he wants to be likeable - in PHM basically Grace and Rocky - he writes that way.
  - Yeah PHM did something that that I often see in Sorkin's work and makes me crazy: every single character has the exact same quippy sense of humor. Even the security guards and buttoned-down scientists
    - Sorkin needs a director to filter down his work or else it's terrible, Fincher knew how to tone it down perfectly.

Worst thing that happened to him is becoming a director. Not because his directing is that terrible, it's that nobody is filtering the scripts.
  - I haven’t seen the movie yet but I read the book a long while ago back before everyone was going crazy for it. I was not particularly blown away by it. I enjoyed it enough but am not a big fan of Andy Wier’s writing voice. I was pretty shocked to learn how universally loved it is when I found it “just ok”.
    - Loving the book while acknowledging that Andy Weir's writing voice is atrocious is a valid position.

Recently I've been hearing from a lot more people who hated the book because of Weir's writing style, but I actually found the film a vindication of him. It proved there was a compelling story under the sometimes cringey writing. And it still worked despite almost completely ditching Weir's signature (the thing he is generally acknowledged to be great at), the procedural problem solving stuff.
    - Weir writes novels with the hopes they'll be picked up as a TV show or movie. It's popcorn fun. 
2. Being more like Arrival would not have really worked, because the movie is fundamentally about Grace’s (physical and emotional) isolation and how his relationship with Rocky turns all that around. He goes from a guy who was forced on a suicide mission that would save his own planet to a guy who sacrifices his own life (intends to, at least) to save Rocky and Rocky’s planet. To that end, it’s crucial to the story that Rocky be a character that *we* as the audience also love and believe is worth dying for. 

The humor is a quick way to get to that, and is definitely a bit too easy/lazy as times. But I also think a dreadfully serious movie wouldn’t work as well for this type of story. And Rocky is adorable so it’s all good
3. I found the movie to be pretty frictionless and basically as deep as a children’s story book.

None of the stakes felt that serious tonally — there is always a bright undercurrent of playful optimism that I think hurts the dramatic stakes. Everything felt too “perfect” and resolved cleanly when in reality, I think anyone in Grace’s shoes would actually just go insane to wake up in the middle of space with 2 dead humans with you. Especially when it’s explicitly shown he has 0 physical or psychological training to be an astronaut. 

A decent time at the movies, sure, but I wasn’t Moved by it honestly. I’m willing to see it a second time in Dolby for a different experience this week (first time in IMAX 70mm), though. 
4. I enjoyed the movie a lot as well, but your point really hits home during the scene where he is told he has to go on the mission. They were basically assaulting and drugging him against his will, and they made it seem like he was being a panicky, immature idiot. I didn't read the book, but was told by many people who did, that it was handled much more seriously in the book.
5. I agree. Entertaining but not a top tier science fiction film. Grade of B for me. Nice to see smart people doing smart things w/o a villain hanging over everything. Too flippant at times which took away from the gravity of the situation. Rocky became too anthropomorphized for my taste.  The science could have been better explained. I enjoyed it for the most part.

## 4. One of the biggest pleasures in contemporary film was realizing part way through that Fincher's The Killer (2023) was a satire

**Score:** 323
**Body:** Once we realized this the film became not only so much more enjoyable - poking subtle fun at many of those who came to it hoping for a slick action/assassin film and were disappointed - it became hilarious. Fassbinder's character is constantly, arrogantly wrong and Internet meme trite in his self-talk. In the first few viewings though - its so good I've watched it many times - t I just thought it was just satirizing Melville's Le Samouraï (1967) but last night I watched The American (2010) and saw so many other touch points like the "in a great shape for a man his age" self-workout scenes (though there is certainly a nod to Brad Pitt's shirt-off moment from Fight Club too), the tourism travelog feels, and the ways The American was probably reflecting the precision-aura of Le Samouraï too. To further flesh it out the humor, as I've commented elsewhere a few months ago:

*When he says that he has to wait until his heartrate beats per minute to get down to 60 (? I forget the number), but then he fires the rifle at something like 68 and misses. Listening to the Smiths Meat is Murder while attempting to assassinate someone. The way he acts like an expert on everything, just like internet commenters (but totally guessing the dog's weight and dose wrong). The rich guy taking a limo to the gym, which is a 5 minute walk away (shown by the phone). The abandoned (Unicorn Finance) WeWork office. The killer eating "keto" (just eating the burger patty from fastfood, if I recall)...The killer is a successful gig-economy worker who lives the fantasy beach house dream. But he's totally lame. Does his yoga, measures his beats-per-minute on his watch, quotes his adages, eats his keto, guesses wrong at everything. He's a very, very subtle (not over the top) Inspector Clouseau. He's a bourgeois killer, like if that ultra, ultra flexible guy in your yoga class was also an international assassin.*

In any case, the satire key really unlocks this film, a correction perhaps of how many who loved Fight Club but did not realize that it was satire about Toxic Masculinity, the very thing they loved it for. And watching George Clooney's The American made it even better. It actually is posing through satire a strong critique of the hyper-Capitalism consumer "subject", opening up the notion that we are all self-satisfied, non-reflective "killers" in our lifestyle.

A recommendation: if you enjoyed The Killer and haven't seen The American, do (its a pretty good slow burn, beautiful film in its own right).

3. You could also look at the movie and I know this is tired, but you could look at it as a metaphor for filmmaking. 

He’s a consummate professional that absolutely has his methods, routine, and style nailed down and even though he’s the best he does still make mistakes and you could even excuse it because things weren’t exactly perfect to start with. But he is also an expert in improvising and planning out contingencies. “It rained on the day so what is the plan for getting this scene inside?” 

His keto adjacent diet also reflects the commonly held practice among experienced crew members to not eat carbs while working because it makes you more tired than it helps and makes you gain weight. (A lot of the job is waiting to get the shot and moving on to the next location very quickly.) 

My favorite expression of this metaphor is how his boss takes two people with completely different styles and just thinks that putting them together at huge expense means it will get the job done. Like calling in whoever’s available and good enough when you need to fix something in reshoots. 
  - To further this idea slightly, I believe the killer missing is a filmmaker putting out a flop or dud, and trying to course correct to prove he can still do his job and isn’t getting rusty. In that way I think it can connect to Fincher directly with Mank being disregarded comparatively to his filmography, showing that he can still get the job done and release a quality product in The Killer.

## 5. Why are there so few movies where the woman is the “monster” and still loved?

**Score:** 308
**Body:** We’ve seen so many stories where a male “monster” (literally or metaphorically) is loved by a woman like the whole Frankenstein dynamic.

But where are the stories where the woman is the monster?

I don’t mean just flawed or morally grey I mean actually unsettling, strange, inhuman, or even frightening in some way… and still genuinely loved or desired.

It feels like those stories are way rarer, and I’m curious why. Is it just a gap in storytelling, or does it say something about how female characters are expected to be perceived?

If you know any movies  that explore this where a woman is truly “monstrous” but still loved 

i’d really appreciate recommendations. 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think "Let the right one in" would be an example of this. She is a monster that slaughters the kids bullies (sure they were about to basically murder the kid as unhinged movie bullies tend to do, but still) and he still loves her.
2. Titane comes to mind. The main character is monstrous in various ways, but other characters desire and sympathize with her. The movie explores desire & identity, so it’s not as straightforward as the male monsters you described, but you should certainly watch it based on your post. 
  - Titane and Ex-machina (which someone else mentioned further down) kind of show where the limits are for female monsters - even if they have monstruous elements you can still more or less perceive them as conventionally attractive women, and their status as objects of desire is a major part of both films.

It's much harder to find gender-reversed examples of the hunchback / beauty-and-the-beast trope where the 'monster' is canonically hideous to others, but becomes loved for their inner character. Even expanding the scope beyond monster movies, the only example I can think of off the top of my head where a male character ends up looking past appearances without any trickery like revealing the nerdy girl was actually secretly hot the whole time is Shallow Hal.
    - You reminded me of the ugly hunchback girl in Witcher. I was really excited to see how they explored her life as a freak, but she got magicked hot right away. Then I was really excited to see how someone who had been hideous that was now conventionally attractive viewed the world and how people treated her. Instead she acted like a hot girl who had always been hot. That show was such a disappointment.
    - I don’t think people should bring up Ex Machina in this context because you’re talking about a machine not a woman. There’s no context in the film that the androids even see themselves in a gendered way. They’re just machines who were created to look like attractive women. Also, clearly the monster in this movie is Oscar Isaac’s character (and to a lesser extent, Domhall Gleason). The android is just trying to exist in the world and not be destroyed. You could make a case that this is analogous to women in movies, since some men see us as objects and not people as well. But that’s a different discussion. Also I love Titane. 
3. the feminist analysis would probably be that society presents men as active and women as passive, but if she's the evil monster and he loves her then she's the active one and he's the passive one, which is a position of reduced agency that men wouldn't be used to and would be uncomfortable with, particularly for a whole story arc.


  - This is my discomfort with a lot of Guillermo Del Toro and (to a lesser, but still present extent), Tim Burton movies. Del Toro does it better but I don't like how both make movies where classically beautiful women become devoted to monstrous men while the films cling to extremely old school, eurocentric views of beauty (especially with the women) (this is a Burton problem mostly) and seem to present women as uniquely emotionally intelligent if you view these films together (I think Del Toro does this more than Burton but also I'm glad he's open about it?)
    - Del Toro gives a woman control there. She is not the helpless victim of her own desires, manipulated by a man. She is actually the one who mediates the relationship between the creature and the outside world. In The Shape of Water, all Elisa has to do is let the authorities into her house to get rid of the creature. She chooses otherwise. That is why I do not mind Del Toro doing that, I like the dynamic.
    - It’s not just limited to those two directors, either. I don’t have examples off the top of my head, but the “insightful female sees what her brash male colleagues cannot” trope goes back quite a ways.
      - I agree 100%, it's a very common trope they're using (Del Toro credits King Kong and Creature from the Black Lagoon as influences here), but I think these filmmakers have made a specific style of woman a part of their as auteur-manship (if that's a word) in a way not many filmmakers have. The only comparisons I can think of are Hitchcock and you could make a case for Woody Allen (who wrote nearly all of his movies as vehicles for two actresses, so I don't know if he counts)

(if you have more examples of directors like this please let me know! I might make a post about this!) (also I should disclaim that I do enjoy plenty of Del Toro and Tim Burton films and don't specifically hate them!)
